data.title_primary: Southern Alps Monitoring of Weather-Influenced Seismicity and Erosion
data.description_primary: Our project focuses on studying seismic activity in New Zealand's Southern Alps/Kā Tiritiri o te Moana by collecting and analyzing new seismic data. We will expand the current seismometer network, develop high-quality earthquake datasets, and investigate environmental signals like landslides and avalanches. By using advanced modeling techniques, we aim to understand how surface changes, such as glacial retreat, influence seismicity and crustal deformation.
data.description_objectives: The primary target of this proposal is New Zealand Southern Alps/Kā Tiritiri o te Moana. Bridging across disciplinary fields in different Earth Science domains, this proposal aims to provide insights into the physical processes causing seismicity in this hyperactive alpine orogen by the acquisition of new seismic data. We will address the project goal via four main objectives: (1) Deployment of nine seismometers to extend the existing seismometer network and a weather station. (2) Generate new high-quality seismicity datasets. We will use state-of-the-art seismological methods to extend the existing earthquake catalogs. In addition, we also aim to identify environmental signals related to phenomena such as landslides, rock falls, and avalanches. (3) Develop mechanical models of the effects of surface loads on crustal deformation. We will examine the effects of glacial unloading (and possibly also variations in pore pressure) in the crust using three-dimensional mechanical models. (4) Interpret models and datasets to characterize the nature of seismicity. We will provide a summary and synthesis of the main findings, along with their implications, to explore the relationships between seismological, hydrological (including ice and water), and geodetic observations and better understand the mechanisms driving seismicity.
data.instrumentation_type: LPR200; Lennartz LE-3Dlite MkII
data.contributor_name: Konstantinos Michailos; Meghan Miller; John Townend; Calum Chamberlain

data.title_primary: Monitoring Maunakea and Kilauea volcanos using DAS
data.description_primary: ARC Centre of Excellence COMBS joint investigation with the W.M. Keck Observatory in Hawaii to run a distributed acoustic sensing experiment on Maunakea, which is located on the Big Island of Hawai'i. We aim to record small, previously undetected earthquakes on the dormant volcano and use these and ambient noise signals to image the volcano structure.
data.instrumentation_type: Nanometrics Centaur; Silixa iDAS 25; GPS Antennas; Power Cables

data.description_primary: A temporary network of seismic nodes focussed on Ōkataina caldera volcano in the North Island of Aotearoa New Zealand. We seek to image the volcano in detail and detect local earthquakes to improve our knowledge of the volcano's structure and processes and the hazard that it poses.
data.description_objectives: Ōkataina caldera volcano is one of two active caldera volcanoes in Aotearoa New Zealand, and it has been as the highest threat volcano in the country. Despite the most recent eruption coming from Tarawera in 1886, the area deep beneath Makatiti and Haroharo is very active: most of the earthquakes beneath Ōkataina caldera happen beneath this area; local earthquake tomography results show Vp and Vp/Vs anomalies (Bannister et al, 2022); and magnetotelluric imaging shows a low-resistivity anomaly arrested at 1–2 km depth beneath Makatiti Dome (Bertrand et al., 2022). The magma reservoir has caused multiple eruptions in the past and drives the geothermal activity we see today. We aim to improve constraints on the location, depth, and state of magmatic and hydrothermal systems through seismic imaging and investigation of local seismicity. This will inform potential unrest and eruption scenarios, improve our response to unrest through better physical models of the volcano, and be communicated to local communities to develop greater preparedness for future volcanic hazards from Ōkataina. We seek to install a dense network of 80 seismic nodal instruments across the Makatiti and Haroharo areas, plus four additional instruments to the southeast to fill in a gap in the national seismic network, for 3–5 months. We will use the data for detailed ambient noise tomography and receiver function analyses to image the shallow crust of the volcano in detail, as well as investigating any local seismicity that happens during the deployment.
data.instrumentation_type: IGU-16HR 3C 5Hz; IGU-16s Portable Data Harvester; IGU-16s Portable Battery Charger

data.description_indigenous_engagement_summar:
We have been speaking with representatives of local Iwi (Indigenous Māori tribal groups) in whose rohe (region) Makatiti and Haroharo lies, including Ngāti Tarāwhai, Ngāti Pikiao, Ngāti Rangithi, Tūhourangi and Ngāti Ronogomai. We have had multiple meetings with representatives of Ngāti Tarāwhai, including the chair. They have expressed their support of the project and will be actively involved in the deployment and management of the network. The proposed project was taken to the board meeting of Te Mana O Ngāti Rangitihi Trust, and they have agreed to the proposed seismometer testing within the Ōkataina caldera: a cultural monitor will observe the work. We have met with the Taiao (environment) lead for Tūhourangi about the project. They have expressed verbal enthusiasm about the project, but it has not yet been confirmed. The chair of Rotoiti 15 trust (see below) is also the chair of Ngāti Pikiao. We continue to build relationships in the region with other people and representatives of other Iwi as recommended by our partners.

The land the seismometers are planned on is managed by three organisations: Rotoiti 15, Māori Investments Limited and the Department of Conservation (DOC). We have permission from each of these organisations to install the network, subject to access and health & safety conditions for the fieldwork. Rotoiti 15 is an Iwi trust that represents Ngāti Pikiao, Ngāti Tarāwhai and Ngāti Rongomai interests. They manage the land that 32 of the proposed sites are on, and road access leading to 11 of the sites on DOC managed land. We have met multiple times with the general manager of Rotoiti 15. Their Taiao board has expressed support for the project and representatives will be actively involved in the deployment of the network. We will present on the project to keep the committee updated on 17th July. Māori Investments Limited manages the land that 27 of the proposed stations are on. The operations manager has confirmed they are in support of us putting the stations in, subject to access, health and safety conditions to be discussed closer to the time. Department of Conservation (DOC) manages the land that 25 of the proposed stations are on. We have applied for permission with DOC, and they have granted us permission for the install.
data.description_indigenous_acknowledgement: All of the conversations around partnership, access and permissions above are ongoing. Parts of the network layout may change as discussions progress about permissions, access, and safety. There will be cultural induction before the work begins, and local guides and cultural observers from these communities coming with the physical science team during the fieldwork.

data.description_primary: The Wairakei (WETA) experiment consists of a short term (~30-60 day) nodal array targeting the Wairakei Geothermal Field. 45 SmartSolo IGU-BD3C-5 broadband nodes and 120 SmartSolo IGU-16HR 3C short period nodes were deployed in September 2023. All data recorded at 250 Hz. The aim of the project is to image the local seismic velocity structures in the top 5 km and detect and characterize local seismicity.

data.title_primary: Seismic Network Around Kangaroo Eyre Yorke
data.description_primary: This deployment of 20 broadband seismometers is intended to fill a gap in seismic coverage in South Australia, along the south-eastern margin of the Galwer Craton. The aim is to better characterise the local seismicity, and to improve seismic imaging of the crust and upper mantle structure beneath this region.

data.description_primary: The Lake George Seismic Nodal Array was a short-term seismic experiment conducted on a then dry lake bed located ~35 km northeast of the Australian capital city, Canberra. The array included 97 three-component SmartSolo sensors recording continuously with 250 Hz sampling rate, and was operated between December 2020 and January 2021 with an average inter-station spacing of 30-40 m.

data.description_primary: The new deployment of ~25 broadband seismometers will provide the opportunity to dramatically improve the rendering of 3-D seismic structure in the crust and mantle lithosphere. The objective of this work is to provide a compilation of 3-D models, which will be an effective characterisation of the structure of the craton and its margins. These data and models will provide improved information for enhanced assessment of seismic ground shaking from regional earthquakes and facilitate an improved understanding of mineral resource potential.

data.title_primary: Morocco - SUD West African Craton Margin Profile
data.description_primary: This project proposes a broadband magnetotelluric (MT) survey along a 185 km transect across the western margin of the West African Craton (WAC), extending from the Dakhla Coastal Basin through the Adrar Souttouf to the Reguibat Ridge. A total of 45 MT stations will be deployed, spaced at approximately 5 km intervals, with tighter spacing of 2.5 km in areas of known mineral prospectivity. The survey targets a geologically diverse region that includes Mesozoic basins, Ediacaran metamorphic complexes, and Archean craton, intersecting key structures associated with rare earth element (REE), gold, platinum group element (PGE), and iron oxide mineral systems. Data will be collected using ANSIR LEMI-423 instruments with Ag-AgCl electrodes and LEMI-120 magnetic coils. This work forms part of a collaboration between the University of Adelaide and Morocco’s Office National des Hydrocarbures et des Mines (ONHYM), and aims to generate new geophysical insights in a frontier area for exploration.
data.description_objectives: The primary objective is to image deep lithospheric structures that may control mineralisation across the WAC’s western margin. The survey seeks to map major crustal faults and shear zones, identify conductive anomalies associated with hydrothermal fluid pathways, and evaluate the relationship between deep structures and known mineral occurrences, particularly those linked to carbonatites, gold systems, and tectonic contacts. Results will be integrated with existing gravity and geological datasets to construct a geodynamic framework for mineral system development in the region. In doing so, the project aims to provide a structural and lithospheric context for exploration targeting and demonstrate the value of MT for deep mineral systems mapping in geologically complex and underexplored terrains.
data.instrumentation_type: LEMI-423; LEMI-120 Magnetic Coils; Borin Stelth Ag-AgCl Electrodes; Field Laptop
data.contributor_name: Khalid Amrouch; Graham Heinson; Mohammed Boumehdi; Ben Kay; Goran Boren
data.organisation_name: National Office of Hydrocarbons and Mines; University of Adelaide; National Office of Hydrocarbons and Mines; University of Adelaide; University of Adelaide

data.description_primary: FMG Resources Pty Ltd (FMG) was successful in securing Round 2 Accelerated Discovery Initiative (ADI) funding for its proposed Integrated Exploration Under Deep Cover: Geophysics, Geochronology, Geochemistry research collaboration project with the University of Adelaide (UoA), where new passive seismic and magnetotelluric data were collected across the Vulcan prospect in South Australia.
data.description_objectives: The scope of the research project was to collect new geophysical, geochemical, and geochronological datasets across the Vulcan prospect. These datasets, in conjunction with exiting exploration data would then be used to improve the 3D characterisation of the mineral system and provide information of the paragenetic framework and physical response developed during multiple fluid events. The outcomes of the project and ongoing processing of datasets will be used to optimise future drilling programs. It will show how geophysical signatures can be linked to the chemical and temporal evolution of a mineral system. In addition, this project will permit researchers to evaluate the spectrum of IOCG mineral system processes from a crustal to prospect/deposit scale, which may be applicable to other prospects/deposits within the Gawler Craton and elsewhere.

data.title_primary: Western Gawler Magnetotelluric & Passive Seismic Project
data.description_primary: The discovery of magmatic Ni-Cu sulphide in the Western Gawler Craton, confirmed the presence of significant, intrusive-hosted magmatic Ni-Cu sulphide in the Fowler Domain. The primary aim of this proposal is to develop a 3D model the Fowler Domain crust from the near surface to the Moho to identify the regional structural controls on emplacement of Ni-Cu-PGE prospective mafic-ultramafic intrusions. This proposal is in a collaboration between Western Areas and the University of Adelaide to complete three regionally-spaced magnetotelluric (MT) and passive seismic transects across key areas of the Fowler Domain. Such new data sets, coupled with legacy MT data, will be used to generate detailed 2D and 3D resistivity models of the Fowler Domain, with resolution from the near surface to the Moho. Passive seismic data will also constrain the cover sequences that can be introduced into the resistivity inversion. The project will integrate and interpret all the new and legacy datasets simultaneously to develop a 3D model with both sections and 3D views as outputs
data.description_objectives: The ADI Western Gawler Project aims to develop a 3D geophysical model of the Fowler Domain crust, from the near surface to the Moho, to identify regional structural controls influencing the emplacement of Ni-Cu-PGE-bearing mafic–ultramafic intrusions. The project seeks to address the challenges of exploring under extensive sedimentary cover and in regions with subdued geophysical responses by applying high-resolution magnetotelluric (MT) and passive seismic techniques. It proposes to deploy three MT transects, covering approximately 140 km of strike length with 82 stations spaced at 2 km intervals, using broadband MT instruments (0.001–10,000 Hz) to image conductivity structures from shallow depths (~10 m) to deep crustal levels. Simultaneously, passive seismic sensors will be installed along the same traverses to provide velocity constraints on cover sequences and crustal layering. These datasets will be integrated with existing geological and geophysical data to refine understanding of the geodynamic evolution of the Fowler Domain and improve exploration targeting. The project also includes a collaboration with CSIRO to characterise magnetic remanence in drill core samples and assess its relationship to mineralisation, enhancing interpretation of magnetic anomalies in areas with complex or low-contrast geophysical signatures.
data.instrumentation_type: IGU-16HR 3C 5Hz; IGU-16s Portable Data Harvester; IGU-16s Portable Battery Charger; LEMI-423; LEMI-120 Magnetic Coils; Borin Stelth Ag-AgCl Electrodes; Field Laptop; Sonnenschein GF 12 63 Y 12V Batteries

data.title_primary: Curnamona Downward Looking Telescope: Curnamona Cube MT Program
data.description_primary: The Curnamona Cube project is deploying 200 broadband magnetotelluric (MT) stations across the Curnamona Province on a 20 km grid, forming part of a broader deep Earth imaging initiative covering 90,000 km² across South Australia and New South Wales. This work is led by the University of Adelaide, in partnership with ANU, the University of Melbourne, Geoscience Australia, the State Geological Surveys, and several industry collaborators. The survey aims to image the electrical resistivity structure of the crust and upper mantle to depths of 300 km, offering a high-resolution view of a geologically complex and underexplored province. Early deployments, completed via helicopter and 4WD from Arkaroola to the SA-NSW border, are already feeding into what will become one of the most comprehensive geophysical datasets in Australia.
data.description_objectives: The MT component of the project is designed to build a three-dimensional resistivity model of the Curnamona Province that will shed light on its crustal architecture and tectonic evolution. By imaging conductive pathways and lithospheric boundaries, the survey will help define deep structures potentially associated with mineral systems, especially in areas obscured by thick sedimentary cover. This data will be critical for mineral explorers, especially given the province’s similarities to nearby resource-rich regions like Mount Isa and the Gawler Craton. The long-term aim is to provide a foundational dataset for integrated studies combining geology, magnetics, gravity, and geochemistry to identify new exploration targets across this greenfields region.
data.instrumentation_type: MTU-5C; LEMI-423; MTU-150 Magnetic Coils; LEMI-120 Magnetic Coils; Borin Stelth Ag-AgCl Electrodes; Field Laptop; Sonnenschein GF 12 63 Y 12V Batteries
data.contributor_name: Graham Heinson; Ben Kay; Goran Boren
data.contributor_position_id: Principal or Chief Investigator;Co-investigator or Collaborator;Co-investigator or Collaborator

data.title_primary: Curnamona Downward Looking Telescope: Curnamona Cube Passive Seismic Program
data.description_primary: Running alongside the MT deployments, the Curnamona Cube project is also installing 200 passive seismic stations across the same 20 km grid. These instruments are being used to record ambient seismic noise and teleseismic events, which will allow researchers to develop detailed models of seismic velocity variations throughout the crust and upper mantle. This dual approach enables a multi-parameter perspective on lithospheric structure, enhancing resolution and interpretability. Additionally, a pilot observatory known as the Curnamona SuperSite is being established near the Kalkaroo deposit. At this site, a smaller grid of seismometers, MT instruments, and heat-flow probes are co-located to allow long-term monitoring and advanced Earth imaging experiments.
data.description_objectives: The seismic component of the project aims to map changes in crustal composition, thickness, and structure across the province, helping to interpret the geodynamic evolution of the region. Using techniques like ambient noise tomography and receiver function analysis, researchers will build velocity models to complement the MT-derived resistivity models. At the Curnamona SuperSite, the long-deployment passive seismic array will provide continuous monitoring to detect subtle signals from deep Earth processes, enabling better understanding of tectonic activity, lithospheric layering, and possible mineralisation pathways. Together, these data will form the basis of the Curnamona Cube, an integrated 3D Earth model to support long-term research, exploration, and national geoscience capability.
data.instrumentation_type: IGU-16HR 3C 5Hz; IGU-16s Portable Data Harvester; IGU-16s Portable Battery Charger; Field Laptop

data.description_primary: The Eyre Peninsula Nodal Array (EPNA) consists of a mixture of SmartSolo IGU-16HR 3C short period nodes and SmartSolo IGU-BD3C-5 broadband nodes. The experiment consists of short-term (~30 day) deployments along the eastern Eyre Peninsula in South Australia, starting in late 2023. The project will target the local seismic velocity structure across the Kalinjala Shear Zone, within the Gawler Craton.
data.description_objectives:
• The H2EX CRC-P aims to explore for natural hydrogen on the Eyre Peninsula in South Australia.
 • The longer-term goal is to conduct several joint MT and passive seismic nodal arrays in 2024 to study the underlying crustal structure, potential sources, and sub-surface pathways for natural hydrogen.
 • Ahead of these larger scale arrays, a smaller pilot study will be undertaken in late 2023 using 20 nodal seismometers along a linear transect crossing key structural and geological boundaries within the study area.
data.instrumentation_type: IGU-16HR 3C 5Hz; IGU-BD3C-5s
data.contributor_name: Caroline Eakin; Chengxin Jiang; Meghan Miller; Louis Moresi; Graham Heinson
data.contributor_position_id: Principal or Chief Investigator;Co-investigator or Collaborator;Co-investigator or Collaborator;Co-investigator or Collaborator;Co-investigator or Collaborator

data.funding_title: CRC-P: Accelerating exploration and extraction of renewable natural hydrogen
data.funding_agency_name: Department of Industry, Science and Resources

data.description_primary: The magnetotelluric component of the CRC-P "Accelerating exploration and extraction of renewable natural hydrogen" sought to characterise the crustal architecture of the study area on H2EX's PEL 691 license to identify potential sources, migration pathways and traps for hydrogen. There were multiple phases of deployment: (a) a 50-km feasibility transect in late 2023, (b) a regional-scale array covering 100 squared kilometres in early 2024, and (c) five infill transects, each spanning 25 - 60 km in late 2024.
data.description_objectives:
Feasibility transect: 
-	High-level 2D resistivity cross-section characterising the main lithologies and structural features across the study area
-	Investigation of data quality in the study area prior to regional survey
Regional array: 
-	Creating a crustal (0 – 40 km) 3D resistivity model of the study area and analysis in relation to the regional resistivity of the Eyre Peninsula
-	Identifying mechanisms for a natural hydrogen reservoir in the Eyre Peninsula through interpretation of geophysical responses.
o	Potential sources include mantle-derived fluids, serpentinization from subducted oceanic crust and radiolysis from radioactive granites; related lithologies have varying MT responses.
o	Potential fluid migration pathways including crustal-scale faults and permeable lithologies historically showing as narrow zones of low resistivity.
Infill transects:
-	Obtain resolution of shallow subsurface structures, particularly potential traps and reservoirs.
-	Increase horizontal resolution across regions of interest to narrow down drilling-prospective areas.

data.description_primary: Short term (30 day) node array targeting the greater Auckland Volcanic Field and the Hauraki Rift to the east. 52 SmartSolo BD3C 5s nodes were deployed May-June 2023 which were supplemented by five additional Trillium Compact 120s stations (AKB*) which will remain installed for an extended period. All data recorded at 250 Hz.

data.description_primary: The Jupiter MT Anomaly Definition Study is a collaborative research project between Havilah Resources and the University of Adelaide, with instrumentation and in-kind support provided by ANSIR and Geoscience Australia. The study focuses on the Kalkaroo region within the Curnamona Province, a geologically significant area concealed by thick sedimentary cover that hinders conventional mineral exploration. The project was co-funded through a $50,000 State Government ADI grant and matched by Havilah Resources, with additional logistical support provided in kind. New magnetotelluric (MT) data were acquired, with helicopter-supported field logistics enabling a dense array of observation sites.
data.description_objectives: The primary objective is to define the architecture of deep mineral systems from source to sink, with a focus on identifying electrically conductive pathways linking the lower crust to the upper crust. The resulting 3D resistivity models reveal a pronounced low-resistivity feature, informally dubbed the "finger of God", that connects a deep crustal conductor to the basement top. This structure shares strong morphological similarities with known IOCG systems in the Gawler Craton and Mount Isa Province, offering critical insight into potential fluid pathways and mineralisation processes. The study not only validates earlier 2D models but also demonstrates the capability of MT to resolve complex subsurface features in regions of limited outcrop. While no commercial products were developed, the workflow and interpretation strategy have significant implications for undercover exploration, and the results are intended for publication in peer-reviewed journals and presentation at conferences. Further MT surveys are planned to extend and build upon these findings.

data.description_primary:
A magnetotelluric (MT) survey is planned across the Kalkaroo Magnetic Complex in South Australia’s Curnamona Province, focusing on the Kalkaroo copper-gold-cobalt deposit and its surrounding domes. The area contains a mix of Proterozoic rock sequences that are known to host mineralisation, with the Kalkaroo Dome and nearby North and Deep Well domes marking key structural features of interest.

By combining audio-frequency (AMT) and broadband (BBMT) methods, the survey will capture images of the crust from near-surface to mantle depths. High-resolution data over the deposit and fault zone, together with broader coverage across the domes, will support 3D resistivity models. These results will be integrated with existing geological and geophysical studies to improve understanding of the region’s mineral potential.

data.description_primary: The Burra MT project aims to investigate the 3D crustal architecture of the Adelaide Rift Complex in South Australia using a scale-integrated magnetotelluric (MT) approach to better understand fluid pathways in post-subduction porphyry copper systems. The project will combine long-period AusLAMP MT data with a new dense grid of broadband MT observations to image the electrical resistivity structure of the crust and upper mantle beneath the Burra region, which is known to host both magmatic and sedimentary-hosted copper mineralisation. Plans include the deployment of 82 broadband MT sites across the Northern Mount Lofty Ranges using LEMI-423 instrumentation from ANSIR and the Geological Survey of South Australia, with an additional 42 sites to be acquired using Phoenix MTU-5C systems to increase resolution and coverage.
data.description_objectives: The objective of this research is to map a whole-of-lithosphere copper system in a post-subduction tectonic setting, using MT to trace the geoelectrical footprint of ore-forming fluids from their deep crustal source to near-surface mineralisation. The study aims to improve understanding of how magmatic hydrothermal fluids are focused and remobilised in intracontinental rift settings, particularly where prior subduction has chemically modified the lithosphere. By resolving key fluid pathways and structural controls on mineralisation, the project contributes to broader efforts to define terrane-scale fertility under cover and to develop predictive exploration models in regions where conventional methods are limited.

data.description_primary: A new broadband magnetotelluric (MT) transect is planned across a ~100 km NW–SE profile within the Delamerian Orogen, targeting a region obscured by Murray Basin sediments. The survey will use a dense array of MT stations to investigate deep crustal and upper mantle structures along the eastern hinge of the Nackara Arc and into the Loch Lily-Kars Belt an area where surface geology offers limited insight. By extending existing AusLAMP coverage with higher-resolution broadband data, the survey will build on recent discoveries of deep conductive features, such as the Nackara Conductor, that appear linked to significant mineral systems across southeast Australia. Instrumentation will be sourced from the AuScope and GSSA pools, with deployment optimised for imaging through basin cover.
data.description_objectives: The aim is to resolve the resistivity structure of the lithosphere in this key transitional zone, improving our understanding of crustal architecture, fluid pathways, and long-lived structural controls on mineralisation. Particular focus will be placed on identifying low-resistivity zones that may signal regions of past fluid movement and metallogenic potential. Results will guide future geoscientific drilling under the MinEx CRC National Drilling Initiative, by helping to prioritise locations where deep lithospheric structures intersect more accessible upper crustal domains. The data will also contribute to broader models of the tectonic evolution of southeast Australia and refine how mineral systems are spatially and temporally linked to deep crustal processes.
data.instrumentation_type: LEMI-423; LEMI-120 Magnetic Coils; Borin Stelth Ag-AgCl Electrodes; Field Laptop; Sonnenschein GF 12 63 Y 12V Batteries

data.title_primary: Marine MT imaging of the Spencer Gulf, South Australia
data.description_primary: A pilot marine magnetotelluric (MT) survey is set to expand the Australian Lithospheric Architecture Magnetotelluric Project (AusLAMP) onto the continental shelf for the first time in South Australia. The survey focuses on the Spencer Gulf, targeting a key data gap along the eastern margin of the Gawler Craton. Twelve MT instruments are planned for deployment along a transect between Tumby Bay and the Yorke Peninsula, in shallow waters ranging from 17 to 40 metres. This collaborative effort, led by the University of Adelaide with partners at the Geological Survey of South Australia, Geoscience Australia, and the Scripps Institution of Oceanography, aims to test the performance of broadband to long-period MT responses in marine environments. The Spencer Gulf transect builds directly on adjacent land-based MT coverage, offering a seamless extension from land to sea across this geologically significant region.
data.description_objectives: The primary objective is to assess the feasibility and quality of marine MT data collection in water depths less than 200 metres, using a hybrid approach that combines seafloor electric field measurements with nearby land-based magnetic data. The pilot study aims to evaluate how effectively corrected marine responses can be integrated with existing land MT models, enabling a continuous geoelectrical image across the continental margin. A key geological aim is to improve understanding of the resistivity structure beneath the Eyre Peninsula, particularly the continuity of crustal-scale faults and conductive Paleoproterozoic metasediments that are prospective for both critical minerals and natural hydrogen. Results from this initial deployment will inform future marine MT extensions into other high-priority offshore areas, contributing to a national-scale lithospheric framework.
data.instrumentation_type: OBEM; LEMI-423; LEMI-120 Magnetic Coils; Field Laptop; Sonnenschein GF 12 63 Y 12V Batteries

data.title_primary: MT imaging near the Kapunda Mine, South Australia
data.description_primary: A field trial is being conducted to assess the feasibility of using modern In-Situ Recovery (ISR) techniques at the historic Kapunda copper mine in South Australia. Located within a fractured rock aquifer in a semi-arid region, Kapunda is recognised as Australia’s first mining town and holds both historical and geological significance. Although the site has been inactive since the 19th century due to the low grade of remaining ore, recent advancements in ISR technology have prompted renewed interest in extracting copper from previously uneconomic deposits. The trial involves controlled injection and recovery of lixiviants, supported by integrated geophysical and hydrogeological monitoring, to evaluate both the technical performance and environmental impact of ISR in this setting.
data.description_objectives: The aim of the study is to evaluate the feasibility, safety, and environmental performance of ISR in a fractured rock setting. Specifically, the project investigates how injected solutions move through the aquifer system and whether there is potential for migration toward nearby surface water bodies like the Light River. By combining stochastic groundwater modelling with geophysical monitoring, the study seeks to determine the residence time and migration pathways of the injectant. A successful demonstration at Kapunda would not only validate ISR as a low-impact method for copper extraction but also provide a real-world model for unlocking stranded assets elsewhere in Australia, offering a scalable approach to future resource development.

data.title_primary: AusArray SA: 3D lithospheric arcitecture of the eastern-central Gawler Craton and metallogenic Olympic Cu-Au Province
data.description_primary: The AusArray SA passive seismic array is designed to map the 3D seismic structure of the eastern-central Gawler Craton, a region largely obscured by cover and home to South Australia’s prospective iron oxide-copper-gold (IOCG) province. A total of 38 broadband stations will be deployed across the region, matching the spacing of the completed AusLAMP magnetotelluric array to enable integrated geophysical analysis. The array includes 28 Nanometrics Trillium Compact 120 s sensors and 10 Trillium Compact 20 s sensors, with instruments sourced from both the AuScope-ANSIR pool and Geoscience Australia. Stations will operate continuously for 18 months, capturing ambient noise and teleseismic earthquake data for high-resolution crust and mantle imaging. This deployment complements ongoing efforts by the Geological Survey of South Australia and ANU to delineate lithospheric features across the craton using passive seismic methods.
data.description_objectives: The project aims to generate a detailed 3D seismic wave-speed model of the crust and upper mantle beneath the eastern Gawler Craton and its margins, with a particular focus on mapping lithospheric architecture relevant to mineral systems. Key targets include crustal and lithospheric thickness variations, fluid pathways, and metasomatic footprints in the mantle that may be linked to IOCG mineralisation. A range of modelling techniques will be applied, including body and surface wave tomography, receiver functions, and shear wave splitting, to capture seismic velocity variations from the upper crust to the lithosphere-asthenosphere boundary. By integrating these results with AusLAMP MT data, the project seeks to identify regions of mineral prospectivity at depth using contrasting geophysical sensitivities to temperature, composition, and fluids.
data.instrumentation_type: Nanometrics Trillium Compact 120s; Nanometrics Trillium Compact 25s; Nanometrics Trillium Compact 120s; LPR200

data.description_primary:
A new magnetotelluric (MT) survey is planned over the Vulcan copper-gold prospect in South Australia to investigate the connection between deep crustal conductors and upper-crustal IOCG-style mineralisation. The work will target a previously mapped vertical conductive feature together with a recently identified natural-source induced polarisation (NSIP) anomaly.

By extending the survey to the northeast and re-occupying selected earlier stations for reference, the program will deliver higher resolution images of the subsurface. The integration of MT and NSIP analysis provides an innovative approach to detecting chargeable zones beneath thick cover, helping refine exploration tools while improving understanding of the geological controls on mineralisation in the region.
data.description_objectives:
The program aims to directly address three key questions central to IOCG exploration under cover:
(1) can natural-source IP responses extracted from MT datasets reliably identify chargeable zones associated with mineralisation?
(2) how do these chargeability features relate spatially to the deep vertical conductive structures previously identified at Vulcan and other IOCG deposits?
(3) what constraints can be placed on the continuity and geometry of the “Finger of God” feature to support a future scientific drilling campaign under the International Continental Drilling Program (ICDP)?
By combining broadband MT and NSIP data, the survey intends to map the electrical architecture from near-surface to depths exceeding 5 km, defining critical zones of alteration, fracturing, and potential fluid flow.
data.instrumentation_type: LEMI-120 Magnetic Coil; LEMI-423; Borin Stelth Ag-AgCl Electrodes; Field Laptop; Sonnenschein GF 12 63 Y 12V Batteries

data.title_primary: Probing the Australian-Pacific plate boundary: Macquarie Ridge in 3-D
data.description_primary: Imaging the 3D structure of the oceanic crust and underlying lithosphere along the Macquarie Ridge Complex is the central focus of this work. Located at the active boundary between the Australian and Pacific plates in the southwest Pacific Ocean, the region is geologically exceptional, being the only known example where typical oceanic crust is exposed above sea level within its original ocean basin. The study is linked to RV Investigator voyage IN2020-V02 and includes the deployment of a broadband ocean-bottom seismometer (OBS) array, along with land-based stations positioned on and around Macquarie Island. Despite the logistical and environmental challenges of operating in the Southern Ocean, data recovery and processing using QuakeMigrate software has enabled the development of a detailed microseismicity catalogue, even under noisy and variable conditions.
data.description_objectives: The objective of this work is to better understand the tectonic processes and fault structures that govern earthquake generation in this complex region, including those responsible for the largest oceanic strike-slip earthquake of the 20th century (Mw 8.2, 1989). By applying waveform-based detection and high-resolution relocation methods (such as GrowClust), the team is producing detailed images of seismogenic faults and investigating temporal changes in earthquake activity. These results are integrated with geodetic data and geological mapping to refine models of crustal deformation and assess how strain is accommodated along this unique boundary. The study will advance global understanding of plate boundary processes and contribute to the broader context of oceanic crust dynamics and large-magnitude intraplate seismicity.
data.instrumentation_type: Guralp Certimus 120s; Guralp CMG-6T 60s; IGG CAS G-type 60s Broadband OBS

data.title_primary: Utilising Distributed Acoustic Sensing and Dark Fibres for Smart Water Asset Monitoring
data.description_primary: A new approach is proposed to monitor the condition of underground water pipelines by repurposing existing telecommunication optical fibre cables as distributed acoustic sensors (DAS). These unused “dark fibres,” which are already widespread beneath urban infrastructure, offer a low-cost and scalable solution for detecting cracks, leaks, and structural faults in water distribution networks. Unlike traditional sensor deployments, which can be expensive and logistically challenging, DAS enables continuous monitoring across long distances with minimal disruption. The use of fibre optic cables transforms passive infrastructure into real-time sensing systems, providing a foundation for smarter, more responsive water asset management in Australia’s aging pipeline network.
data.description_objectives: The main goal is to develop and demonstrate a DAS-based monitoring system that delivers early detection of pipe faults with high sensitivity and spatial resolution. By integrating DAS with advanced signal processing and intelligent analytics, the system will convert continuous acoustic data into actionable insights on pipeline condition. This will support predictive maintenance strategies and reduce the risk of costly failures. The project also aims to validate the effectiveness of DAS for urban water monitoring at scale, offering a transformative, cost-effective alternative to conventional smart water networks. Outcomes will position Australia as a leader in the deployment of fibre-enabled sensing infrastructure for critical public utilities.
data.instrumentation_type: Silixa iDAS 24
data.contributor_name: Martin Lambert; Wei Zeng; Benjamin Cazzolato
data.organisation_name: University of Adelaide; University of Adelaide; University of Adelaide

data.description_primary: The Southland region of New Zealand lies at the peripheral edge of the Australian-Pacific plate boundary, and at the juxtaposition of multiple geologic terranes that were accreted onto the Gondwana margin during the Mesozoic. However, constraints on seismicity in this region, and the possible influence of its complex geologic history on modern day deformation processes, are limited by the sparse coverage of the permanent GeoNet network (station spacing ~100 km). To address this knowledge gap, we deployed 19x short period seismometers between October 2022-2023 as part of the Southland Otago Seismic Array (SOSA). Supplemented by GeoNet stations and a temporary deployment in neighbouring Fiordland (Fiord22), the reduced SOSA station spacing (10-30 km) has provided the first opportunity to study and characterise microseismicity in this region.
data.instrumentation_type: LPR200; Lennartz LE-3Dlite MkII
data.contributor_name: Jack Williams; Donna Eberhart-Phillips; Sandra Bourguignon; Mark Stirling

data.description_primary: This project focuses on investigating the recent surge in earthquake activity in central Australia, particularly around Kati Thanda - Lake Eyre. Despite being a stable continental interior, this region experiences frequent intra-plate earthquakes, the causes of which remain poorly understood. A new AuScope-funded deployment of 40 seismic stations, including broadband and short-period sensors, will expand seismic coverage across the Lake Eyre Basin and adjacent Gawler Craton. This network will fill a critical gap in the national seismic array, enabling high-resolution imaging of sub-surface crustal structures hidden beneath extensive sedimentary cover. The seismic stations will be strategically positioned in a challenging and remote environment, including across the Simpson Desert, to capture valuable data that would otherwise be inaccessible.
data.description_objectives: The main aim of the project is to improve our understanding of the crustal structures and geological boundaries that control earthquake activity in central Australia. By applying a suite of passive seismic imaging techniques, including body and surface wave tomography, receiver functions, and ambient noise analysis, the project will detect hidden faults, weak zones, and fluid pathways that may influence seismicity and mineral systems. A particular focus is the suspected continental boundary between the Gawler Craton and the Lake Eyre Basin, which may be linked to both elevated seismic hazard and significant mineral prospectivity. Ultimately, the project seeks to inform hazard assessment and support exploration efforts by building a predictive model that connects deep crustal structure, seismic behaviour, and resource potential.
data.instrumentation_type: LPR200; TerraSAWR; Nanometrics Trillium Compact 120s; Lennartz LE-3Dlite MkII
data.contributor_name: Caroline Eakin; Michelle Salmon; Meghan Miller
data.organisation_name: Australian National University; Australian National University; Australian National University
data.funding_title: ARC Discovery Early Career Researcher Award (DE190100062)
data.funding_agency_name: Australian Research Council

data.description_primary: A passive seismic investigation is planned across key tectonic domains of Western Australia to improve resolution of crustal and upper mantle structure in a region that remains poorly imaged compared to the rest of the continent. The survey will utilise broadband three-component seismic stations deployed along the WT and WV transects across the Yilgarn Craton, with additional WR and WP sites providing extended coverage. These arrays will record teleseismic events for receiver function analysis, enabling the mapping of seismic velocity variations, Moho depth, and discontinuity structure from the Pilbara Craton, across the Capricorn Orogen, into the Yilgarn Craton. By using closely spaced stations in targeted profiles alongside more widely spaced regional coverage, the survey design allows for both local-scale and broad lithospheric imaging.
data.description_objectives: The study aims to produce a high-resolution seismic model of the crust and upper mantle that can delineate major tectonic boundaries, identify relict structures, and assess variations in Moho depth and character across the study area. Key objectives include testing for the continuity of crustal blocks across the orogens, resolving the geometry and composition of deep structures, and improving constraints on tectonic evolution models for the Pilbara-Capricorn-Yilgarn system. The results will be integrated with other geological and geophysical datasets to refine regional geodynamic interpretations and to support exploration targeting in Western Australia.
data.instrumentation_type: Guralp CMG-40T 30s; Guralp CMG-3ESP 30s; Streckeisen STS-2; Nanometrics Orion recorder; Reftek 72A-07 recorder
data.contributor_name: Anya Reading; Brian Kennet
data.organisation_name: Australian National University; Australian National University

data.description_primary: A 25 km segment of dark fiber from the Australian Light Infrastructure Research Testbed (ALIRT) in Melbourne is being repurposed to create a dense Distributed Acoustic Sensing (DAS) array for passive seismic imaging in an urban setting. The fiber, originally installed for research use between photonics labs at RMIT and Monash University, now supports over 6,000 DAS channels spaced at 4 metres. The array runs from Melbourne’s central business district out toward the eastern suburbs, closely following arterial roads, tram lines, and local infrastructure, including the Victoria Bridge. Physical channel locations were partially verified through field tap tests and maintenance access points, with the remainder interpolated to within about 20 metres accuracy. The dataset captures a complex urban environment with varied noise sources, road traffic, and structural coupling conditions, offering a unique opportunity to explore the use of DAS for passive imaging in highly developed areas.
data.description_objectives: The primary objective of this work is to develop and demonstrate a robust workflow for passive seismic imaging in urban environments using dark fiber DAS. The study addresses key challenges such as variable ground coupling, irregular array geometry, and strong diurnal variation in anthropogenic noise. By refining techniques for urban noise analysis and source distribution modelling, the project aims to improve DAS-based imaging resolution and reliability. The outcomes will help establish best practices for urban DAS deployments and highlight the potential of leveraging existing fiber networks to enable low-cost, high-density seismic sensing in cities worldwide.
data.instrumentation_type: Silixia iDAS 2
data.contributor_name: Voon Hui Lai; Meghan Miller
data.contributor_position_id: Chief Investigator; Co-investigator
data.organisation_name: Australian National University; Australian National University

data.description_primary: A passive seismic survey is proposed across the Pilbara Craton, Capricorn Orogen, and Paterson Orogen to investigate the deep crustal structure of this geologically significant region. Broadband seismic stations, spaced at approximately 40-45 km, will record teleseismic waveforms to enable receiver function analysis and seismic velocity modelling. These methods are well suited to identifying key features such as the Moho, mid-crustal discontinuities, and lateral variations in crustal thickness. The survey will target areas where subsurface structure remains poorly constrained and where deep geological boundaries are not well defined from existing surface mapping or geophysical datasets.
data.description_objectives: The primary aim is to refine the location and nature of crustal boundaries between major tectonic units and to detect relict structures preserved at depth. This includes characterising the geometry of the Moho across the orogens, identifying variations in seismic discontinuities along the transect, and linking these observations to surface geology and tectonic history. The results will contribute to broader efforts to understand crustal assembly, lithospheric evolution, and the relationship between deep structure and mineral systems in northwestern Australia.
data.instrumentation_type: Guralp CMG-3ESP 30s; Streckeisen STS-2; EarthData PR6-24 recorder
data.contributor_name: Anya Reading; Brian Kennett; Hrvoje Tkalčić
data.organisation_name: University of Tasmania; Australian National University; Australian National University
data.funding_title: ARC Discovery Grant (DP0342618)
data.funding_agency_name: Australian Research Council

data.description_primary: A regional seismic monitoring program is planned for northwestern Australia to investigate the causes and characteristics of seismicity in one of the most tectonically complex and historically active parts of the continent. Despite hosting Australia’s largest recorded earthquakes, the current seismic record for this region is sparse. The geological framework includes multiple Archean and Proterozoic cratons, orogens, and large-scale fault systems, many of which remain poorly understood at depth. By deploying a dense array of broadband seismometers, the survey will enable high-precision earthquake detection and imaging of crustal structure across both onshore and offshore zones. This will support an improved understanding of neotectonics, basement reactivation, and the potential influence of natural resource extraction on local seismicity
data.description_objectives: The primary goals are to map the distribution and frequency of small-to-moderate seismic events, calculate focal mechanisms, and resolve the present-day stress field across the region. Data from the array will help identify active structures, assess the role of inherited basement faults, and clarify how seismicity relates to tectonic boundaries between major geological provinces such as the Pilbara Craton, Capricorn Orogen, and Pinjarra Orogen. The study also aims to distinguish between natural and potentially anthropogenic seismic sources, with implications for infrastructure risk, petroleum production, and regional hazard models. Ultimately, the results will contribute to a better understanding of intraplate seismic processes and inform national efforts in hazard preparedness and geodynamic modelling.
data.instrumentation_type: Guralp CMG-40T 30s; Reftek 72A-07 recorder
data.contributor_name: Myra Keep; Brian Kennett; Phil Cummins
data.organisation_name: University of Western Australia; Australian National University; Geoscience Australia

data.title_primary: Magnetotelluric investigation of the Vestfold Hills and Rauer Group, East Antarctica
data.description_primary: The Prydz Bay MT project, located in East Antarctica is aimed at imaging a suspected suture zone beneath the Sørsdal Glacier. The survey area spans the Vestfold Hills and Rauer Group, two terranes with contrasting geological histories that are thought to have been juxtaposed during the assembly of East Gondwana. A series of MT stations will be deployed along three helicopter-supported transects extending inland from the Davis research station, covering approximately 200 line-kilometres. Due to the unique challenges of working in polar conditions, the survey will use MT systems with preamplifiers designed to overcome the high contact resistance of ice. Stainless steel electrodes will be arranged in 100 m dipoles with magnetic sensors capturing both fluxgate and induction coil data, optimised for data collection in a high-noise, cold-climate environment.
data.description_objectives: The primary goal is to determine whether magnetotellurics can reliably image sub-ice geological structures in coastal East Antarctica and to investigate the electrical signature of a proposed suture between the Rauer Group and Vestfold Hills. The survey will focus on identifying large-scale resistivity contrasts that may indicate the location of this tectonic boundary and its relationship to regional metamorphism and deformation during East Gondwana assembly. In addition, the project aims to refine best practices for MT deployment in Antarctic conditions by evaluating the impact of wind-blown static noise, magnetic source field characteristics, and electrode performance in ice. Insights from this work will inform future deep-field MT deployments elsewhere in the continent.
data.instrumentation_type: EDL; Fluxgate; LEMI-120 Magnetic Coils; Stainless Steel Electrode Plates; Preamplifiers; Earth Data PR6-24 Loggers
data.contributor_name: Jarred Peacock; Kate Selway; Graham Heinson; Alan Collins; Goran Boren
data.organisation_name: University of Adelaide; University of Adelaide; University of Adelaide; University of Adelaide; University of Adelaide
data.funding_title: AAD Grant (2771); ARC Linkage Project (LP0774891); ARC Discovery Project (DP0988263)
data.funding_agency_name: Australian Antarctic Division; Australian Research Council; Australian Research Council

data.description_primary: A high-resolution broadband magnetotelluric (MT) survey is proposed across the southern Eyre Peninsula to refine the structure, extent, and origin of one of Australia’s most prominent crustal conductivity anomalies, the Eyre Peninsula Anomaly (EPA). This feature, initially discovered in the 1980s through geomagnetic depth sounding (GDS), spans several hundred kilometres and has since become one of the most studied conductive zones in the country. While GDS and early MT data established the anomaly’s lateral scale, uncertainties remain regarding its depth, continuity, and geological source. The new survey will complement the legacy datasets with modern MT instrumentation and dense station spacing (~2 km), deployed along an 80 km east–west transect where two-dimensional responses are expected to be clearest. This approach aims to resolve the structure at crustal scale, from the surface to depths of at least 20 km, over an area known for significant graphite deposits, iron and base metal mineralisation, and structurally complex Paleoproterozoic rocks.
data.description_objectives: The main aim is to precisely define the depth, thickness, and electrical properties of the EPA conductor and assess its spatial relationship to mapped shear zones and known mineral systems. By acquiring high-resolution MT data in a geologically well-constrained area, the survey will enable robust inversion modelling to quantify bulk conductance and geometry of the anomaly. Integration with previous GDS and AusLAMP results will allow for consistent interpretation across scales, improving our understanding of how the EPA fits within the broader lithospheric framework of the Gawler Craton. The refined model will help identify whether conductivity is driven by graphite-rich metasediments, interconnected fluids, or structural deformation, and will provide a valuable geophysical dataset to guide future mineral exploration across the region.
data.instrumentation_type: LEMI-423; LEMI-120 Magnetic Coils; Borin Stelth Ag-AgCl Electrodes; Field Laptop; Sonnenschein GF 12 63 Y 12V Batteries
data.contributor_name: Graham Heinson; Ben Kay; Goran Boren; Lachlan Loader; Kate Brand; Stephan Thiel
data.organisation_name: University of Adelaide; University of Adelaide; University of Adelaide; University of Adelaide; University of Adelaide
data.funding_agency_name: Geological Survey of South Australia; AuScope

data.description_primary: Deep crustal conductors observed in tectonically stable regions present an enduring puzzle in solid Earth geophysics. One area of particular interest lies beneath the Mt Gambier subprovince of the Newer Volcanics Province (NVP) in southeast Australia, where long period MT & GDS imaging has revealed a low-resistivity zone extending across the mid to lower crust. Interpreted as evidence of fluid-assisted alteration and potentially partial melt, this conductor offers a rare opportunity to explore links between electrical conductivity, fluid transport, and the deeper mechanisms driving intraplate volcanism. A new MT survey is proposed to increase resolution across this feature and investigate whether the anomaly reflects rehydrated deep crust, partial melt, or carbon-rich lithologies. The survey will expand on earlier datasets by infilling critical gaps, especially along the southern margin of the NVP, to build a more complete 3D resistivity model of this dynamic region.
data.description_objectives: The proposed MT acquisition targets two key objectives: first, to constrain the physical and chemical origins of the crustal conductor beneath the Mt Gambier subprovince and, second, to test the broader hypothesis that such anomalies reflect relic deep-crustal fluid pathways. Particular attention will be paid to distinguishing between conductive phases associated with past magmatism, such as interconnected melt or alteration halos and those linked to metasomatic reworking of the lower crust. By integrating new broadband MT data with existing long-period and GDS datasets, the project aims to deliver a robust geoelectrical framework that can inform geodynamic models of intraplate volcanism in southeast Australia. The results will also provide critical input into natural hazard assessments for the NVP and enhance understanding of conductivity anomalies across stable continental interiors more broadly.
data.instrumentation_type: LEMI-423; LEMI-120 Magnetic Coils; Borin Stelth Ag-AgCl Electrodes; Field Laptop; Sonnenschein GF 12 63 Y 12V Batteries
data.contributor_name: Graham Heinson; Sam Jennings; Ben Kay; Goran Boren; Kate Brand; Stephan Thiel
data.organisation_name: University of Adelaide; University of Adelaide; University of Adelaide; University of Adelaide; University of Adelaide

data.title_primary: Seismic Investigations of Lithospheric Transitions between the Northern and Southern Australian Cratons
data.description_primary: A broadband seismic array is planned to investigate lithospheric-scale transitions between major cratonic blocks in central Australia, focusing on the structural links between the Gawler Craton, Musgrave Block, Arunta Block, and Mt Isa Block. The deployment will target key intercratonic suture zones where previous studies have identified anomalously slow seismic velocities in the upper mantle, extending to depths of at least 75 km. By spanning multiple tectonic domains and connecting northern and southern Australian cratonic regions, the configuration is designed to characterise variations in seismic velocity, attenuation, and discontinuity structure that reflect the processes of continental assembly and intracratonic reworking
data.description_objectives: The primary goals are to determine the nature and depth extent of lithospheric transitions between these cratonic regions, assess the controlling factors behind observed low-velocity anomalies in intercratonic zones, and test whether such features propagate with depth. Through joint velocity and attenuation tomography, combined with receiver function analysis, the study will identify variations in lithospheric architecture linked to past orogenic events such as the Alice Springs Orogeny, and provide new constraints on the formation and evolution of Australian intercratonic suture belts. The results will improve understanding of continental amalgamation processes and contribute to broader models of lithospheric structure across the Australian continent.
data.instrumentation_type: Guralp CMG-3ESP
data.contributor_name: Sara Rawlinson; Brian Kennett
data.organisation_name: Australian National University; Australian National University
data.funding_agency_name: AuScope

data.description_primary: A portable broadband seismic array is planned for deployment across the Mount Isa region and surrounding areas of northwestern Queensland to investigate crustal and upper mantle structure in one of Australia’s most significant mineral provinces. Instruments will be positioned on a grid with ~50 km spacing to record seismic waves from distant earthquakes, enabling high-resolution seismic velocity imaging to depths of 50-100 km. The study area contains the Gidyea Suture zone within the Mt Isa succession, an important but poorly understood crustal feature potentially linked to large-scale mineralisation. Previous geophysical surveys, including magnetics, gravity, magnetotellurics, and seismic refraction, have highlighted pronounced structural and conductivity anomalies, but the geometry, composition, and tectonic history of mid-crustal features remain unresolved.
data.description_objectives: The primary aim is to generate new tomographic images that define the geometry and properties of crustal and lithospheric structures beneath the Mt Isa region, with a focus on the Gidyea Suture and its relationship to known mineral systems. Specific goals include mapping seismic velocity variations associated with major geological boundaries, assessing crustal heterogeneity relevant to mineralisation models, and constraining the depth extent and continuity of mid-crustal conductive zones. The dataset will be integrated with existing magnetic, gravity, and magnetotelluric results to refine models of the region’s tectonic evolution and improve exploration targeting for lead, silver, copper, and zinc resources.
data.instrumentation_type: Lennartz LE-3Dlite MkII
data.contributor_name: Brian Kennett; Herb McQueen
data.organisation_name: Australian National University; Australian National University
data.funding_agency_name: AuScope

data.title_primary: Magnetotelluric broadband survey of Western Tasmania
data.description_primary: Broadband MT surveys are planned across key geological boundaries in western and northwestern Tasmania to investigate the lithospheric structure and its relationship to mineralisation. The traverses will cross the contact between Precambrian Tyennan basement and the Cambrian-Devonian Dundas-Fossey Trough, a metalliferous terrane hosting significant volcanic-hosted massive sulfide deposits. Tasmania’s west presents both opportunities and challenges for MT acquisition, from complex tectonic history, rugged terrain, dense vegetation, and limited road access. MT measurements, made using broadband PR6-24 Earth Data Loggers paired with LEMI-120 magnetic coils, will capture high-resolution geoelectric data to depths of tens of kilometres, providing an essential link between AusLAMP long-period datasets and local-scale geological information.
data.description_objectives: The survey aims to resolve the geometry, depth extent, and electrical properties of major crustal boundaries and mineralised systems in western Tasmania. Key objectives include: (1) mapping variations in resistivity that correlate with the Tyennan-Dundas contact and associated structures; (2) integrating broadband MT results with AusLAMP data to improve lithospheric-scale models; (3) identifying potential fluid pathways or alteration zones linked to VHMS mineralisation; and (4) refining 3D geological models to support mineral exploration and tectonic interpretation. The resulting dataset will enhance understanding of crustal architecture in this geologically significant region and provide new insights into the tectonic and metallogenic evolution of Tasmania.
data.instrumentation_type: Earth Data PR6-24 Loggers; LEMI-120 Magnetic Coils
data.contributor_name: Tom Ostersen; Anya Reading; Graham Heinson
data.organisation_name: University of Tasmania; University of Tasmania; University of Adelaide

data.title_primary: Ostler Fault Quakes Array, New Zealand
data.description_primary: The Ostler fault is one of the most active faults in the region east of the central Alpine fault, and underlies major New Zealand infrastructure. The Ostler quakes network will deploy 12 temporary seismographs to obtain accurate locations for very small magnitude earthquakes that are not observed by the sparse permanent network. This will enhance understanding of the deep Ostler fault and help identify other seismically active structures.
data.description_objectives: The Ostler fault is a prominent structure along the margin of the Southern Alps and Central Canterbury tectonic domains, near the junction with the Otago tectonic domain. In recent studies related to the New Zealand National Seismic Hazard Model, it shows ~2 mm/yr geologic slip rate and ~2 mm/yr geodetic slip deficit rate. Geologic mapping shows several major strands along the length of the active fault zone, and previous trenching focused on the northern fault section. This project will include paleoseismic trenching of the southern fault section. The long-term seismicity from the permanent network shows a broad relatively dense patch of seismicity across the Ostler fault region. However there has only been one permanent station (LBZ) in the region, and the earthquake depths are poorly constrained. The Ostler quakes network will be able to record small magnitude seismicity, with accurate hypocentres. This will give reliable seismogenic depths for hazard models, and will better constrain the Ostler fault structure and seismic activity on adjacent potentially active fault structures.
data.instrumentation_type: LPR200; Lennartz LE-3Dlite MkII
data.contributor_name: Donna Eberhart-Phillips; Mark Stirling
data.organisation_name: Earth Sciences NZ; Otago University
